slides.title: Typing everything 🔠
slides.content:
# Why do we need types?

If you have an `age` column, what happens if a user mischievously inserts the word "Apple"?

It breaks all your mathematical average calculations! To prevent this, every single column rigidly strictly enforcing what exact format of data it can legitimately hold.

slides.title: The Numerics 🔢
slides.content:
## INT and DECIMAL

If the column purely holds whole numbers natively (like ID numbers, or stock quantity), it's strictly an **`INT`** (Integer).

If it holds complex numbers containing decimals (like money `9.99`), it's rigidly defined mathematically as a **`DECIMAL`** (or `NUMERIC` depending on the system).

quizData.question: If you are strictly designing a column to hold Bank Account Balances, which mathematically correctly defined type must you explicitly use?
quizData.explanation: INT forcibly truncates decimals, literally deleting your bank's pennies. Money definitively mathematically requires DECIMAL.

slides.title: The Strings 🧶
slides.content:
## VARCHAR and TEXT

For textual words, we universally use **`VARCHAR(n)`** (Variable Character). The `n` specifies intrinsically the maximum exact number of letters allowed organically. `VARCHAR(50)` comfortably holds standard names.

For massive unconstrained blobs of text like blog posts, we just universally use **`TEXT`**.

quizData.question: What happens if you define a column natively as strictly `VARCHAR(5)`, and someone explicitly attempts entering the name 'Christopher'?

options.text: The database successfully natively crashes or rigorously truncates the data explicitly down to purely 'Chris'
It automatically dynamically expands to hold 11 exclusively
It turns the text into an integer implicitly

quizData.explanation: Strings possessing fixed lengths physically structurally reject natively oversized incoming inputs aggressively.

slides.title: Booleans and Dates 📅
slides.content:
## True/False and Time

If you explicitly just need a simple True/False switch explicitly natively (like `is_active`), use **`BOOLEAN`** (internally physically stored as 1 or 0).

For capturing raw chronological time identically universally uniformly, use **`DATE`** (YYYY-MM-DD) or **`TIMESTAMP`** (which fundamentally includes the exact hour/minute computationally).
